items.question: It's the law: you ___ wear a seatbelt.
explanations: Correct! A strong rule takes 'must': 'You must wear a seatbelt'.
'Mustn't' would forbid wearing one.
'Don't have to' would mean it's optional.
'Haven't to' is not correct English.

items.question: This is a hospital — you ___ smoke here.
explanations: 'Don't have to' means it's optional, not forbidden.
Correct! Prohibition takes 'mustn't': 'You mustn't smoke here'.
'Haven't to' is not correct English.
'Must smoke' would be an obligation to smoke.

items.question: Great news — the entry is free, so you ___ pay.
explanations: 'Mustn't pay' would mean paying is forbidden.
Correct! No necessity takes 'don't have to': 'You don't have to pay'.
'Must pay' is an obligation to pay.
'Have to pay' is an obligation to pay.

items.question: My boss says I ___ work this Saturday.
explanations: Correct! Obligation from outside (the boss) takes 'have to': 'I have to work'.
Never add 'to' after 'must'.
'Mustn't' would forbid working.
'Am must' is not correct English.

items.question: Yesterday I ___ leave early to catch a train.
explanations: 'Must' has no past form.
Correct! Past obligation takes 'had to': 'I had to leave early'.
'Have to' is present; this is past ('yesterday').
Never add 'to' after 'must'.

items.question: She ___ finish the report; it's due at noon.
explanations: Correct! With he/she/it, 'have to' becomes 'has to': 'She has to finish'.
'Have to' doesn't agree with 'she'.
Never add 'to' after 'must'.
'Haves to' is not a word.

items.question: Which sentence means it is NOT necessary?
options: You don't have to tell anyone.
explanations: 'Mustn't tell' means telling is forbidden.
Correct! 'Don't have to tell' = it isn't necessary (but you can).
'Must tell' is an obligation.
'Have to tell' is an obligation.

items.question: Correct this: 'You must to book a table.'
explanations: Correct! 'Must' takes the base verb with no 'to': 'You must book'.
Modals never add -s.
Use the base verb, not '-ing', after a modal.
'Must to book' wrongly adds 'to'.

items.question: ___ I wear a tie, or is it optional?
explanations: Correct! Questions with 'have to' use 'do': 'Do I have to wear a tie?'
'Must to' is never correct.
'Have I to' is old-fashioned and rare.
'Am I have to' is not correct English.

items.question: The children ___ shout in the library. It's a rule.
explanations: 'Don't have to shout' would mean shouting is optional.
Correct! A rule forbidding it takes 'mustn't': 'They mustn't shout'.
'Must shout' is an obligation to shout.
'Haven't to' is not correct English.
